from dataclasses import dataclass
import re

@dataclass
class Triple:
    subject: str
    predicate: str
    object: str

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-START

def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    """
    Convert a list of building‑related triples into a fluent, natural‑language sentence.
    """
    # Mapping from predicate to a readable phrase (more concise for common predicates)
    verb_map = {
        "architecturalStyle": "has an architectural style of",
        "buildingStartDate": "was started in",
        "completionDate": "was completed in",
        "floorCount": "has",
        "location": "is located in",
        "cost": "costs",
        "floorArea": "covers a floor area of",
        "architect": "was designed by",
        "owner": "is owned by",
        "formerName": "was formerly known as",
        "height": "rises to",
        "buildingType": "is a",
        "developer": "was developed by",
        "tenant": "has a tenant",
        "isPartOf": "is part of",
        "country": "is in",
        "currentTenants": "currently houses",
        "address": "has the address",
        "inaugurationDate": "was inaugurated on",
        "birthPlace": "was born in",
        "deathPlace": "died in",
        "bedCount": "has",
        "region": "is in the region of",
        "state": "is in the state of",
        "website": "has website",
        "yearOfConstruction": "was constructed in",
        "NationalRegisterOfHistoricPlacesReferenceNumber": "has NRHP reference number",
        "addedToTheNationalRegisterOfHistoricPlaces": "was added to the NRHP on",
        "significantBuilding": "is known for the building",
        "governingBody": "is governed by",
        "leader": "leader is",
        "chancellor": "has chancellor",
        "governmentType": "has government type",
        "capital": "has capital",
        "language": "speaks",
        "leaderTitle": "has leader title",
        "currency": "uses currency",
        "ethnicGroup": "has ethnic group",
        "origin": "originates from",
        "significantProject": "is known for the project",
        "foundationPlace": "was founded in",
        "demonym": "has demonym",
        "numberOfRooms": "has",
        "keyPerson": "has key person",
        "architecture": "features architecture",
        "postalCode": "postal code is",
    }

    # Group triples by the subject to avoid repeating the subject in every clause.
    from collections import defaultdict
    subject_groups = defaultdict(list)
    for t in triples:
        subject_groups[t.subject].append(t)

    # Map objects that are also subjects (to enable merging)
    object_is_subject = {t.object for t in triples if t.object in subject_groups}

    # Keep track of subjects that have been merged into another sentence
    merged_subjects = set()

    def describe_entity(ent: str) -> str:
        """
        Create a short descriptive phrase for an entity that appears as an object
        of another triple. Uses a limited set of predicates to keep the phrase short.
        """
        parts = []
        for tr in subject_groups.get(ent, []):
            # Only handle a few informative predicates for the sub‑description
            if tr.predicate in {"birthPlace", "deathPlace", "leader", "architect", "owner", "location"}:
                verb = verb_map.get(tr.predicate, "has")
                obj_clean = tr.object.strip('"').strip("'")
                # If the object itself is another subject, avoid deep nesting
                if obj_clean in subject_groups:
                    continue
                parts.append(f"{verb} {obj_clean}")
        if parts:
            # Join with commas, prepend the entity name
            return f"{ent}, " + ", ".join(parts)
        else:
            return ent

    sentences = []
    for subject, group in subject_groups.items():
        # Skip subjects that have been merged into another sentence
        if subject in merged_subjects:
            continue
        parts = []
        handled = set()

        # Helper to clean quoted objects
        def clean_obj(o: str) -> str:
            return o.strip('"').strip("'")

        # Special predicates that require inverted phrasing or possessive forms
        # Use a mapping to generate “The <phrase> of <subject> is <object>” style sentences
        possessive_map = {
            "capital": "capital",
            "language": "language",
            "birthPlace": "birth place",
            "deathPlace": "death place",
            "leaderTitle": "leader title",
            "demonym": "demonym",
        }
        for tr in group:
            if tr.predicate in possessive_map:
                phrase = possessive_map[tr.predicate]
                obj_clean = clean_obj(tr.object)
                # For language predicate, drop the redundant word “language” from the object if present.
                if tr.predicate == "language":
                    obj_clean = re.sub(r"\s+language$", "", obj_clean, flags=re.IGNORECASE).strip()
                # For ethnicGroup predicate, ensure proper article usage.
                if tr.predicate == "ethnicGroup":
                    # If object already starts with an article or is plural, keep as is; otherwise prepend "the ".
                    if not re.match(r"^(the|a|an)\b", obj_clean, flags=re.IGNORECASE):
                        obj_clean = f"the {obj_clean}"
                parts.append(f"The {phrase} of {subject} is {obj_clean}")
                handled.add(tr.predicate)
            elif tr.predicate == "floorArea":
                # Produce natural phrasing for floor area
                obj_clean = re.sub(r"[()]", "", clean_obj(tr.object)).strip()
                parts.append(f"The floor area of {subject} is {obj_clean}")
                handled.add(tr.predicate)
            elif tr.predicate == "ethnicGroup":
                # Natural phrasing for ethnic groups
                parts.append(f"One of the ethnic groups of {subject} are {clean_obj(tr.object)}")
                handled.add(tr.predicate)
            elif tr.predicate == "buildingStartDate":
                parts.append(f"Construction of {subject} began in {clean_obj(tr.object)}")
                handled.add(tr.predicate)
            elif tr.predicate == "inaugurationDate":
                parts.append(f"The inauguration of {subject} was on {clean_obj(tr.object)}")
                handled.add(tr.predicate)
            elif tr.predicate == "leader":
                # Invert subject and object for natural phrasing
                parts.append(f"{clean_obj(tr.object)} is the leader of {subject}")
                handled.add(tr.predicate)
            elif tr.predicate == "keyPerson":
                # Invert for key person
                parts.append(f"{clean_obj(tr.object)} is the key person at {subject}")
                handled.add(tr.predicate)

        # Preferred ordering of common attributes for readability.
        order = ["location", "height", "architect", "buildingType", "completionDate"]
        for pred in order:
            if pred in handled:
                continue
            for tr in group:
                if tr.predicate == pred:
                    verb = verb_map.get(pred, "has")
                    clause = f"{verb} {clean_obj(tr.object)}"
                    parts.append(clause)
                    handled.add(pred)
                    break

        # Add remaining predicates that were not handled above.
        for tr in group:
            if tr.predicate in handled:
                continue

            # Special handling for height to produce natural phrasing
            if tr.predicate == "height":
                obj_clean = re.sub(r"[()]", "", clean_obj(tr.object)).strip()
                if "metre" not in obj_clean.lower():
                    clause = f"has a height of {obj_clean} metres"
                else:
                    clause = f"has a height of {obj_clean}"
                parts.append(clause)
                handled.add(tr.predicate)
                continue

            verb = verb_map.get(tr.predicate, "has")
            obj = clean_obj(tr.object)

            # Special handling for count predicates to use appropriate nouns
            if tr.predicate == "floorCount":
                clause = f"{verb} {obj} floors"
            elif tr.predicate == "bedCount":
                clause = f"{verb} {obj} beds"
            elif tr.predicate == "numberOfRooms":
                clause = f"{verb} {obj} rooms"
            # Special handling for country to add article when appropriate
            elif tr.predicate == "country":
                # Add "the" for country names that conventionally take an article
                if any(keyword in obj for keyword in ["States", "Kingdom", "Republic", "Union", "Islands", "Federated"]):
                    clause = f"{verb} the {obj}"
                else:
                    clause = f"{verb} {obj}"
            else:
                clause = f"{verb} {obj}"
            parts.append(clause)

        # Append contextual clause for language of location/country if available
        extra_clause = ""
        for tr in group:
            if tr.predicate in {"location", "country"}:
                obj = clean_obj(tr.object)
                for sub_tr in subject_groups.get(obj, []):
                    if sub_tr.predicate == "language":
                        lang = clean_obj(sub_tr.object)
                        lang = re.sub(r"\s+language$", "", lang, flags=re.IGNORECASE).strip()
                        extra_clause = f", where the language is {lang}"
                        break
                if extra_clause:
                    break

        # Build the sentence for this subject.
        if parts:
            # If the first part already mentions the subject (e.g., “X is the leader of Y”), avoid duplicating the subject.
            first_part = parts[0]
            # Detect patterns where the subject appears at the end after “of” or is already the leading noun.
            if re.search(rf"\bof {re.escape(subject)}\b$", first_part, flags=re.IGNORECASE) or first_part.lower().startswith(subject.lower()):
                sentence = ", ".join(parts) + "."
            else:
                sentence = f"{subject} " + ", ".join(parts) + "."
        else:
            sentence = subject + "."

        # Insert extra clause before final period if it exists
        if extra_clause:
            sentence = sentence.rstrip(".") + extra_clause + "."

        sentences.append(sentence)

    # Combine sentences for multiple subjects.
    final_text = " ".join(sentences)
    # Capitalize the first character of the whole output.
    if final_text:
        final_text = final_text[0].upper() + final_text[1:]
    return final_text

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END
